_comment: MUST stay in sync with scripts/synthesis/pilot-classify-thinkers.py:ANCHOR_IDS — same 8 slugs, same order.
reasoning: Hayek is canonical to the classical-liberal tradition: The Road to Serfdom and The Constitution of Liberty anchor the case against central planning. Nobel laureate economist, longtime professor (LSE, Chicago, Freiburg). Foreign-born but the classical-liberal canon reads him as core; foreignness is captured by the nationality field, not tradition or canon_status.

Rajaji is foundational to the Indian classical-liberal canon. Founded the Swatantra Party in 1959 as an explicit free-market opposition to Nehruvian planning, served as Governor-General and Chief Minister of Madras, edited Swarajya. Removing him leaves a hole at the centre of the post-independence liberal tradition.

Naoroji is foundational to the Indian constitutional-liberal tradition — first Indian MP in the British Parliament, three-time Congress president, author of Poverty and Un-British Rule in India which established the drain theory. The corpus references him heavily as a constitutional-moderate counterweight to revolutionary nationalism.

Roy is the founding figure of the Indian social-reform tradition: founded the Brahmo Sabha (1828), campaigned against sati, championed women's education. Removing him leaves a hole at the headwater of nineteenth-century Indian liberal thought. tradition is social_reformer rather than classical_liberal because his programme was primarily religious and social reconstruction.

J.R.D. Tata is canon-adjacent rather than core. He embodied liberal practice — opposing the License Raj, defending private enterprise — but he was not a theorist or editorial voice. canon_status is extended (not core) because removing him weakens the practice-side of the archive without leaving a hole in the intellectual canon. tradition is practice because his contribution is industrial and managerial.

Justice Khanna's lone dissent in ADM Jabalpur (1976) — that the right to life and liberty cannot be suspended even during Emergency — is a touchstone of Indian constitutional liberalism. He resigned from the Supreme Court when superseded. canon_status is extended (not core) because the corpus engages him through this single landmark moment rather than a sustained body of writing.

Mukesh Ambani appears in the corpus as a present-day industrialist referenced in coverage of Indian economic reform — not as an ideological figure himself. canon_status is referenced because removing him would not weaken the archive's editorial spine. tradition is practice (industrial leadership) rather than any doctrinal value.

Nehru is classified non_liberal because his economic programme (central planning, public-sector dominance, the License Raj) is the counterposition the Indian liberal tradition defines itself against in this corpus. This is NOT an editorial endorsement of judgements about Nehru the man — it is a structural classification: the archive engages his ideas as the foil. canon_status is referenced because he appears throughout as that foil.
